speaker: interviewer
text: It's rolling. Stand by for speed. OK. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Are you going to read it now, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Usually, I read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 13th, 1996. The survivor's name is Irene Rozenfeld Boris. My name is Clifford Davids. The interview is taking place in Brookline, Massachusetts, United States of America, in the English language. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] May I have your name and spelling, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Irene, I-R-E-N-E, Boris, B-O-R-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can I have your name at birth and the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-- Chaja Rozenfeld, C-H-A-J-A R-O-Z-E-N-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever had any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Chaja Gitl Jachnes. That's my mother and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me spelling for that, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaja, C-H-A-J-A, Gitl, G-I-T-L, Gitl, Jachnes, J-A-C-H-N-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can I have your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age is 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and spelling and the country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopol, K-O-S-T-O-P-O-L, and this is Poland, was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Boris. Mrs. Boris, let's start out with some of your earliest childhood memories. What-- what might those be?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was memories who they didn't want me to remember, because I was the sixth child born. And my father passed away soon after. He was sick from the first world war, because he was hidden in-- for the Russian, I guess, what it was this-- the First World War. And he developed tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Symcha, S-Y-M-C-H-A, and my mother's was Gitl, G-I-T-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned you had brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how many brothers and sisters you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one I remember was Abraham. He was the fourth child. And then was Szlema, [? Szloma ?]. And I was the sixth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister, Babl-- B-A-B-L. That's Babl. That's what they used to call her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The other one, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Kostopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the city of Kostopol near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopol was a medium-sized city, which in the 1930s, in the late '30s, became a bigger city because of the-- they found some stones there. They used to. And it came a large Polish population there to work. And it became a prosperous city. We had a railroad with a station. We had a hotel. We had three-- three synagogues and a shopping area, and a big outside market, which was on a Tuesday.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a large Jewish population living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the time, Kostopol was part of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was part of Poland till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about what your mother and father did for a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father, I don't remember. But my mother, we had some-- my mother-- we had people working for my mother. And my mother used to sell foot-ware. And were some Ukrainians, some Jewish. And they worked, and my mother used to sell them. She used to prepare all the buying and this. And she was a good business lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a business that your father was in before he passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so. I'm not so sure, because he was sick for a long time that I didn't have [? access ?] to talk to my father too much because he had tuberculosis for quite a few years. And as I was the youngest and so, so they kept apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have a shoe store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where it was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was from the house and-- what was the-- Aptaska 18. That's what was the-- the address.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name-- the street name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-P-T-A-S-K-A, 18 in the Aptaska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this unusual to have a store out of a-- out of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Absolutely no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house in a commercial--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --district?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were a lot of the stores in the area operated by Jewish families, as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this located within the-- in the very heart of Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, in the old part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother and-- brothers and sisters work--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the store, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many employees did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had about 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Was, I guess, a few. I don't remember exactly if I'll say, but a few Jewish and most, and a few Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were three synagogues in the city. Tell me a little bit about your religious background growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: My religious background was like 90% of the city were Orthodox. Not ultra-Orthodox, but Orthodox, like observing Sabbath, holidays, kosher. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Observant, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents living in Kostopol, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- I remember just my grandmother on my father's side. She lived in the same house we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jachna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-C-H-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit more about growing up as the youngest child in this family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was over-protected because my-- my mother had bad experience, whatever, with the first one. And I was-- by the time, I was the only girl home. And since my father passed away, my mother just put all here on me. She was a very good mother, I have to say, good schooling. She gave us the best of schooling she could in this time. And we always had food on the table. And beside this, my mother had in Boston, around Boston, she had four sisters. And she had a mother here. So they really send us a lot of money, just to keep it up, because my mother gave up the business. She became very ill. She gave out the business, so my aunts and my grandmother here supplied us with all the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when she had to give up the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was-- oh, it was probably about 12 till then, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did life--

speaker: interviewee
text: No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did life-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No more, no. And then I became very ill. I had like a kidneys infection in this time. So, like I said, I was over-protected. But my mother's family from Boston told her in a letter that don't worry about money. Even they were poor people here. But they always sending like $50 a month. Everybody, you know, contribute, I think. When When your mother's business closed, it was strictly because of her illness. [INAUDIBLE] yes, strictly illness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did life change for you after the business closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't change too much. No, it didn't change too much, no. A matter of fact that my mother had help. A cousin of my mother's came from a little village, and she stayed with us. And she helped out with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaja too. Your name was Chaja. And the only thing my mother believed was a strong believer in education. This I remember. And my brother couldn't read so good. Even in Europe, she had a tutor coming in every week. And I'm-- for her, schooling was the most important thing in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about your years in school in Kostopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no-name. This time was one school for everybody. And then my brothers, and then we went to a special teacher to learn Hebrew. And Hebrew was start in the schools too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- were there primarily Jewish students in the public school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was a mix.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the relations like between you and your fellow students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1938, was pretty good. They had no differences. We went to school. We went to, like, was a Polish holiday, a national holiday. We went to the synagogue. And was the cantor there chanting, you know, bless the government and everything. In 1939, start being a little bit older. They said-- they start putting up some signs. Don't buy from a Jew. Buy from us. And this was mostly in the Catholic section where the Polish church was. And they say, everybody should go to his own. And little things started. And they knew-- they-- they tried to avoid this because nobody believed it, that this could happen, because we lived together, mostly. Except it was a place like rest of the city that most Ukrainian people lived. And they lived like for themselves. But Polish and Jewish, and it was German that they mostly liked together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a lot of-- you did not have a lot of interactions with Ukrainians in your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't. It was-- you didn't have really till-- till the war and till '41, I think. That's what they showed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned starting in 1939, things started getting more difficult for the Jews. Could you expand on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't start. It-- when the Russian came in. Oh, '39 before the Russians came in it was already-- you could feel the anti-Semitism. You start feeling it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways could you start feeling it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, they start advertising don't buy from a Jew. And really, you felt it when you went-- went out on the street. You start feeling it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did they advertise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just plain, putting up signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is it who put the signs up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my-- my opinion, personal. It was on the Catholic church, because they owned a lot of property. And they owned stores. And they had like a section, mostly Polish people lived there. And-- and it was not Polacks, nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- what do you base your suspicion that it was the-- the church on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it-- I don't say it's the church. It's near the church because they own property there. They own stores. And it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice a change in the relations between yourself and the-- and the Poles--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, much, much, much.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in 1939? And can-- you were, in 1939, you were approximately--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did it change for a girl of 14 or 15?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- when you didn't go out so much outside in the evening because they started throwing glasses. And there, you know? They somebody wore glasses, and so. Little by little, you felt it. And you-- you know, you were like isolated a little from the rest of population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to maintain friendships with all of your friends after that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost, almost, yeah. Not exactly, because I was out of school, and it started the summer. And the air wasn't clear all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you graduate from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, there were seven grades. But the curriculum was much tougher than here. Seven grades, it was like high school here. And it was the summer the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: The summer of 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working that summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you and your family done the summers prior to that when you were out of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, almost nothing. We used to go in the woods and pick up some mushrooms over there. Well, and you know. And you belonged to a Jewish organization, whatever it was there. And friends, and that's all. Saturday, you used to walk around the city. It's like was the usual, you know, time in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Jewish organizations. Did your family, or did you or your brothers belong to one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah we went to-- I was-- they call-- used to call Bital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it's B-I-T-A-L. And it was a better-- I didn't know. It was probably my friends and whatever there. And then was a [? Czomer ?] [? Raziel ?] was [INAUDIBLE]. My brothers belonged to the [? Czomer ?] [? Raziel, ?] because most of the boys belonged there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that for me, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Czomer, C-Z-O-M-E-R, [INAUDIBLE], R-A-A-Z-I-E-L. That's I would spell it. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, 100%.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your brothers intending on going to live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, no. It was just the most social. It was a social organization that-- it was a place to gather, that young people gathered. And they had meetings and lectures and all the things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of cultural events were going on inside Kostopol, either for the Jewish community or in general for the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had theater, not as here, but we had. We had a movie. And most of the-- we had lectures from certain people who came in, most on the-- from the Zionist organizations. They used to have lectures. And they tried to persuade a lot of people going into Israel. And a lot of them went. And they had a kibbutz. It's K-I-B-U-T-Z. And a lot of young-- I would say 20 years old, or so, they went to work on the kibbutz. And they live on hard work. And from over there, if it was a occasion going to Israel, they all went. So this was a big event that some 10 people or so went to Israel. And they went to Israel to work on the-- you know, [INAUDIBLE], whatever, a kibbutzing. They went to a kibbutz in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which kibbutz it was that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --associated with Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where in the country it was, where in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't. But I still have two girls who I met in Israel. And they were the one they went to a kibbutz. They're not alive anymore, but they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you attended these lectures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was actually giving the lecture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who record them. But it was the-- the one who was, you know, from the organization. He was chosen, and he arranged it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in 1939, you mentioned previously, things started to worsen in terms of the relationship between the Jews and the others in Kostopol. In what way did the lectures with these Jewish organizations reflect that? Or how were they involved in-- in that kind of situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not too much they could do. It was not involvement. The only thing they started, the Volksdeutsche. They took him out and they send them to Germany. [INAUDIBLE] of course, they wanted to go. They went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- I'm sorry, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess that's German who were born in Poland. That's the way I think of. In-- starts being a little bit. You could-- you know, you could smell it in the air. Then came one, she was-- her name was [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, that's what I heard. And she was in the Senate. And she start looking for little troubles, like the slaughtering of cows shouldn't be the way it is, the ritual slaughtering. And then a lot of things that she objected. And this was the time when Hitler came to rise. So she came to rise to her position this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure who this person is you're referring to, this woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somebody in the-- the Polish Senate, [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know either. I mean, if I say, I know from remember just things we used to said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as-- as the-- the months drew towards September of 1939 during the summer time, did things progressively get worse leading up to the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We could feel it that something's going to happen. But if they-- the invasion came in, we went away to our little village and we stayed there about a week till things starts cooling down. And then they said that's not the Germans, but the Russians going came in. So then they came back to our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the-- of this village you went to for a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know how you spell it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you made aware of these changes? Were you able to read newspapers? Were you able to listen to the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't in the newspapers so much. It was the mouth-to-mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were people who lived in other areas in Germany, perhaps, who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who-- I mean, they didn't know so much what went on, like the Kristallnacht, or so. I didn't hear. Maybe people who were older or more knowledgeable of those things, they probably hear. But I didn't. But you could smell the air wasn't clean anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any discussions within your family about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a neighbor who was a [INAUDIBLE] guy. And he wore glasses. And he went out one night, and they broke his glasses. But everybody thought it's somebody who's just, you know? But nobody were thinking about what happened, that this is going to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there-- are there any other events in the summer of 1939 before September that you want to relay to us that made you think that it might become dangerous living in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that-- that summer, did you ever feel for your life or ever expect what would have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the-- if you remember, the first time you became aware on September 1st that the Germans had invaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this time, the Russians were there in--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry the Russians were where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The were in our city. They took-- this part of Poland went to the Russians. And the other part were the-- the Germans. So a lot of the people came to our city from the other part, from large-- from all the other cities they came to-- to the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: 99% Jews. And life was rough, and it was really, if you didn't have connections, from food or whatever, everyone, like no more stores. So every storekeeper tried to accumulate some shoes and this. And during the period, you traded for food and other things. And then when the Russians came in, it was bad, but not life-threatening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me backtrack a second. Tell me the first time you saw a Russian troop or troops marching through the streets. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still in the-- in the village. And then they said that the Russian troops are coming. Well, you couldn't go out. They told you to stay in the-- in the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is-- Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. And they brought a lot of-- nothing happened really too extreme, they should kill us or no. Nothing happened. And they tried to tell you that things are going to be fine and that the Germans wouldn't come anymore here. But-- and this ended. I mean, politics, the Russians didn't like to discuss politics. It was part of their business.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that a lot of Jews from the-- from the west--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --moved east into your village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you engage in discussions with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just went, not knowing where they go. But they just left, you know, kept them here. And they thought they most safe here, which a lot of them went back because no work, and no food, whatever they they took for them. It was tough, very rough for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them stay with you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come. Nobody really occupied our house, no. But they had-- it was a few hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a big, big house, with apartments all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time in-- in early September of 1939, who was living at your house, what family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, me, and two brothers and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother lived in the same house, but she had your own bedrooms and-- and kitchen. And the other was rented to our tailor. I don't remember even his name. He had three rooms. And there was another two rooms rented to somebody next door, which was a rich man, [? Spivak. ?] And was rented to them because they lived next door. They had their business. And they rented from us two rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your brothers at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers were home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your whole family was living there in addition to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the tenants.

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived-- my mother and three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] how long were the Russians in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were till-- till '41. [INAUDIBLE]. That's-- is-- [INAUDIBLE] that's the 22nd of June the Germans invaded Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about that approximate-- that's somewhat less than a year that the Russians were there before the Germans came in. What was life like, and how did it change during that year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed the things that food wasn't as it was before. You had to stay in line for everything. And it was just like temporary, everything. I went to Russian school, which I learned a little bit. You had to learn how to [? speak ?]. And then I wanted to go, and I went. It was an Institute. [SIGHS] I don't remember even, like in a laboratory, to be a-- to work in a laboratory.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the summer, already, the Germans came in. So-- but I went to learn the-- the language a little. So it was mostly Ukrainian that they taught us, two things, Ukrainian and Russian. I never learned the Ukrainian, but Russian, I learned. Where were the troops garrisoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, the troops?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did all the Russian troops who were occupying Kostopol live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were on Kostopol, they had barracks there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you know, I don't remember. I know they used to take and they had like a public bath. So they used to take them every day another-- used to march to the bathtubs, you know, to take a bath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a very real presence every day in the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, they had. They had. They marched in the city. And you were comfortable with the Russian army. They made you comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they in no way were violent with any of the people living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, absolutely not. No, absolutely no. They made you comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they-- were they not only friendly, but did they talk with the townspeople, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they relay--

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to trade, you know, buy some things to send home. And there were a lot of Russian Jews who were in the army. And they were in the-- the higher. So like Saturday, Sunday, they used to come and look for Jewish girls. And, I mean, they were very friendly, you know? It was like, yeah. And some of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were a young, single Jewish girl at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I wasn't this time ready yet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your-- how did your family support themselves during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, my mother accumulated a lot of leather goods. And we traded. How, I don't know. I mean, I wasn't involved in those things. Life wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Boris, we're going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine. Whatever you want to do.
